chosen_title: Prosecutors used ChatGPT logs as evidence in the Palisades fire trial
label: AI
headline: Your Chats Can Convict You
image_prompt: A dimly lit courtroom scene viewed from near the judge's bench, looking down at a defendant's table where a single open laptop displays a glowing chat interface with a blinking cursor — the only source of light in the room. The surrounding courthouse woodwork is dark mahogany, old and imposing, with scattered legal papers and a gavel resting just out of focus in the foreground. A bright accent glow radiates from the laptop screen, casting cold blue-white digital light across an empty wooden chair and the polished table surface, creating a stark contrast between the ancient institution of law and modern surveillance technology. The lower third of the frame fades into deep shadow.
tweets: A man is on trial for arson in LA. Prosecutors built their case partly using his ChatGPT conversation logs. Your AI chat history can be subpoenaed. Let that sink in. 🧵

Jonathan Rinderknecht was charged with starting a fire on New Year's Day 2025 in Los Angeles. That fire became one of the deadliest wildfires in the city's history. Investigators needed to prove not just that he did it — but that it was intentional.

Prosecutors built their case from three sources:
• iPhone location data placing Rinderknecht at the scene
• Security camera footage capturing his movements
• ChatGPT conversation logs from before the fire was set

The ChatGPT logs are the part that should make you pause. OpenAI stores your conversations server-side. Like texts, emails, or browser history, they're a digital record — and courts can compel tech companies to hand them over with a valid legal order.

People treat AI chatbots like a private diary — asking things they'd never type into Google, assuming it feels more personal, more sealed off. It isn't. The privacy equation is identical: if it lives on a server, it's potentially discoverable by law enforcement.

Your AI isn't a confessional. It's a timestamped log with a subpoena address.
